texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide emissions from power generation and industrial sources are the primary cause of anthropogenic climate change. To achieve signi fi cant and meaningful reductions in carbon emissions several carbon mitigation strategies must be implemented globally, one of which is carbon capture and storage (CCS) [1]. For CCS to be viable, the strategy requires a capture technology that has a low parasitic load on the industrial process, is cost e ff ective and has a small equipment footprint. Membrane gas-solvent contactors are a hybrid technology of solvent absorption and membrane separation that has been demonstrated to meet these objectives [2,3]. The technology involves the transfer of CO2 from the fl ue gas through a hollow- fi ber membrane, where it is chemically absorbed into a solvent (Fig. 1). This takes advantage of the highly selective nature of solvent absorption technology, while the membrane acts to physically separate the solvent and gas phases. This enables much higher CO2 mass transfer into the solvent
texts.text: Carbon dioxide emissions from power generation and industrial sources are the primary cause of anthropogenic climate change. To achieve signi fi cant and meaningful reductions in carbon emissions several carbon mitigation strategies must be implemented globally, one of which is carbon capture and storage (CCS) [1]. For CCS to be viable, the strategy requires a capture technology that has a low parasitic load on the industrial process, is cost e ff ective and has a small equipment footprint. Membrane gas-solvent contactors are a hybrid technology of solvent absorption and membrane separation that has been demonstrated to meet these objectives [2,3]. The technology involves the transfer of CO2 from the fl ue gas through a hollow- fi ber membrane, where it is chemically absorbed into a solvent (Fig. 1). This takes advantage of the highly selective nature of solvent absorption technology, while the membrane acts to physically separate the solvent and gas phases. This enables much higher CO2 mass transfer into the solvent

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas-solvent contactors are a hybrid technology of solvent absorption with membrane separation that achieves e ffi cient and compact carbon dioxide capture. Here, we report on a successful pilot plant trial of membrane contactor technology undertaking post-combustion carbon dioxide capture from fl ue gas generated by an Australian black coal fi red power station. The pilot plant utilised membrane contactors to undertake CO2 absorption into 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) and the subsequent solvent regeneration stage to produce a pure CO2 product. The pilot plant trials identi fi ed a commercially available non-porous poly dimethylsiloxane composite hollow fi ber membrane as the most suitable for both CO2 absorption and solvent regeneration. The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient for CO2 absorption across the membrane into the solvent was comparable to laboratory results, enabling a recovery of > 90% CO2 from the fl ue gas. Over time the mass transfer coe ffi cient decreased because of both solvent dilution and some MEA loss, which reduced the enhancement the reaction provides to mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer. The overall mass transfer of CO2 from the solvent into the steam sweep during solvent regeneration was greater than that observed in the laboratory for the same temperature. The energy demand of the pilot plant was higher than for conventional CO2 capture technology, given the pilot nature of the process, lack of energy integration and thermal losses from uninsulated membrane modules. Accounting for these factors, the energy duty of the membrane contactor process was evaluated to be less than 4.2 MJ/kg of CO2 captured. Critically, the pilot plant demonstrated the viability of membrane contactor technology for post-combustion carbon capture on an industrial scale.
texts.text: Membrane gas-solvent contactors are a hybrid technology of solvent absorption with membrane separation that achieves e ffi cient and compact carbon dioxide capture. Here, we report on a successful pilot plant trial of membrane contactor technology undertaking post-combustion carbon dioxide capture from fl ue gas generated by an Australian black coal fi red power station. The pilot plant utilised membrane contactors to undertake CO2 absorption into 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) and the subsequent solvent regeneration stage to produce a pure CO2 product. The pilot plant trials identi fi ed a commercially available non-porous poly dimethylsiloxane composite hollow fi ber membrane as the most suitable for both CO2 absorption and solvent regeneration. The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient for CO2 absorption across the membrane into the solvent was comparable to laboratory results, enabling a recovery of > 90% CO2 from the fl ue gas. Over time the mass transfer coe ffi cient decreased because of both solvent dilution and some MEA loss, which reduced the enhancement the reaction provides to mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer. The overall mass transfer of CO2 from the solvent into the steam sweep during solvent regeneration was greater than that observed in the laboratory for the same temperature. The energy demand of the pilot plant was higher than for conventional CO2 capture technology, given the pilot nature of the process, lack of energy integration and thermal losses from uninsulated membrane modules. Accounting for these factors, the energy duty of the membrane contactor process was evaluated to be less than 4.2 MJ/kg of CO2 captured. Critically, the pilot plant demonstrated the viability of membrane contactor technology for post-combustion carbon capture on an industrial scale.

texts.orig: phase to be achieved than through traditional solvent absorption [4 -6]. As such, the equipment size for carbon capture can be reduced signi fi cantly, while the fl exibility and modular nature of membrane technology enables the capture process to be easily accommodated into limited spaces. In addition, the separation of the solvent and fl ue gas phases eliminates foaming and reduces liquid channelling; two major operating issues in solvent absorption columns.
texts.text: phase to be achieved than through traditional solvent absorption [4 -6]. As such, the equipment size for carbon capture can be reduced signi fi cantly, while the fl exibility and modular nature of membrane technology enables the capture process to be easily accommodated into limited spaces. In addition, the separation of the solvent and fl ue gas phases eliminates foaming and reduces liquid channelling; two major operating issues in solvent absorption columns.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactor technology is also able to replace the solvent regeneration stage, commonly known as stripping [7 -9]. This is partially achieved by using steam as a sweep gas on the permeate side of the membrane contactor under vacuum conditions [10]. The reduced partial pressure on that side of the membrane draws CO2 from the solvent, enabling separation to occur without a solvent phase change, and hence achieve regeneration conditions at a lower temperature than conventional desorbers. This ability to avoid substantial vaporisation of the solvent reduces the energy demand of the membrane contactor regeneration process, subsequently reducing the parasitic load of

texts.orig: carbon capture on power generation.
texts.text: carbon capture on power generation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The authors have developed a novel membrane contactor process that has demonstrated potential for e ffi cient carbon capture in a postcombustion carbon capture scenario [11 -14]. The novel membrane contactor process has been successfully demonstrated at the laboratory scale and undertaken two prior limited pilot plant trials of the process, as part of the CO2CRC ' s H3 and Mulgrave projects [15,16]. Here, the authors report on the successful large-scale pilot plant trials of the membrane contactor process undertaking CO2 capture and solvent regeneration continually for an extended period at a black coal fi red power station in New South Wales, Australia. A similar pilot plant process has been reported where porous poly(ether ether ketone) (PEEK) membranes, surface modi fi ed with a functional per fl uoro oligomer, were used to capture CO2 from the fl ue gas of a coal- fi red power station into methyldiethanolamine solvent, followed by successful solvent regeneration [17]. The study presented here di ff ers by utilising a non-porous membrane based on an asymmetric polydimethylsiloxane layer which physically separates the gas and solvent phases. This separation of phases enables a less rigid control of the transmembrane pressure, as well as reduces the likelihood of solvent entrainment and gas bubbling, which is possible in porous membrane systems with fl uctuating feed gas pressures. The purpose of these trials was to demonstrate the successfully implementation of membrane contactor pilot plant technology for CO2 capture and to facilitate process optimisation of the technology.
texts.text: The authors have developed a novel membrane contactor process that has demonstrated potential for e ffi cient carbon capture in a postcombustion carbon capture scenario [11 -14]. The novel membrane contactor process has been successfully demonstrated at the laboratory scale and undertaken two prior limited pilot plant trials of the process, as part of the CO2CRC ' s H3 and Mulgrave projects [15,16]. Here, the authors report on the successful large-scale pilot plant trials of the membrane contactor process undertaking CO2 capture and solvent regeneration continually for an extended period at a black coal fi red power station in New South Wales, Australia. A similar pilot plant process has been reported where porous poly(ether ether ketone) (PEEK) membranes, surface modi fi ed with a functional per fl uoro oligomer, were used to capture CO2 from the fl ue gas of a coal- fi red power station into methyldiethanolamine solvent, followed by successful solvent regeneration [17]. The study presented here di ff ers by utilising a non-porous membrane based on an asymmetric polydimethylsiloxane layer which physically separates the gas and solvent phases. This separation of phases enables a less rigid control of the transmembrane pressure, as well as reduces the likelihood of solvent entrainment and gas bubbling, which is possible in porous membrane systems with fl uctuating feed gas pressures. The purpose of these trials was to demonstrate the successfully implementation of membrane contactor pilot plant technology for CO2 capture and to facilitate process optimisation of the technology.

texts.orig: 2. Theory
texts.text: 2. Theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall objective of both CO2 absorption and rich solvent regeneration is to maximize the CO2 fl ux through the membrane into the respective permeate streams; for absorption this is maximizing CO2 fl ux from the fl ue gas into the solvent and for regeneration this is maximizing the CO2 fl ux from the rich solvent into the steam sweep. The fl uxes of CO2 can be described as:

The fl uxes of CO2 can be described as:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where Kov is the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient (de fi ned by the gas (G) or solvent (L) phases), and LM is the log mean di ff erence of the mass transfer driving force along the membrane module. For the absorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 mole fraction in the solvent bulk (xCO2) and the equilibrium mole fraction of CO2 in the solvent (x*CO2), dependent on the partial pressure of CO2 in the fl ue gas entering and exiting the module. For the desorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 partial pressure in the gas phase (pCO2) and the equilibrium CO2 partial pressure of the loaded 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent at that concentration and temperature (p*CO2) across the module length. The equilibrium CO2 mole fraction and partial pressures were calculated by Aspen Plus (v8.8), given the variations in MEA solution concentrations and operating temperatures of the pilot plant trials.
texts.text: where Kov is the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient (de fi ned by the gas (G) or solvent (L) phases), and LM is the log mean di ff erence of the mass transfer driving force along the membrane module. For the absorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 mole fraction in the solvent bulk (xCO2) and the equilibrium mole fraction of CO2 in the solvent (x*CO2), dependent on the partial pressure of CO2 in the fl ue gas entering and exiting the module. For the desorption process, the driving force for mass transfer is between the CO2 partial pressure in the gas phase (pCO2) and the equilibrium CO2 partial pressure of the loaded 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent at that concentration and temperature (p*CO2) across the module length. The equilibrium CO2 mole fraction and partial pressures were calculated by Aspen Plus (v8.8), given the variations in MEA solution concentrations and operating temperatures of the pilot plant trials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient is the key parameter, as it strongly dictates the fl ux across the membrane and enables comparison between di ff erent membrane contactors systems. This coe ffi cient is a function of mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer, mass transfer through the membrane active layer, mass transfer through the membrane porous support and the mass transfer through the gas boundary layer, and is written as a summation of mass transfer resistances [9]:

This coe ffi cient is a function of mass transfer in the solvent boundary layer, mass transfer through the membrane active layer, mass transfer through the membrane porous support and the mass transfer through the gas boundary layer, and is written as a summation of mass transfer resistances [9]:

texts.orig: where kg, kps km and kl are the gas, porous support, membrane and liquid side physical mass transfer coe ffi cients respectively, H the dimensionless Henry ' s Law constant, d0, di and dIn are the outer, inner and logarithmic mean diameters of the membrane fi ber and E is the enhancement factor for the CO2 -solvent reaction (absorption or regeneration). For most membrane contactor systems, the major resistance to mass transfer is either the membrane active layer or the solvent boundary layer [12]. Both resistances can be reduced; for the membrane active layer by reducing the thickness and transitioning to ultra-thin membranes and for the solvent by increasing the mixing within this boundary layer.
texts.text: where kg, kps km and kl are the gas, porous support, membrane and liquid side physical mass transfer coe ffi cients respectively, H the dimensionless Henry ' s Law constant, d0, di and dIn are the outer, inner and logarithmic mean diameters of the membrane fi ber and E is the enhancement factor for the CO2 -solvent reaction (absorption or regeneration). For most membrane contactor systems, the major resistance to mass transfer is either the membrane active layer or the solvent boundary layer [12]. Both resistances can be reduced; for the membrane active layer by reducing the thickness and transitioning to ultra-thin membranes and for the solvent by increasing the mixing within this boundary layer.

texts.orig: 3. Membrane contactor pilot plant
texts.text: 3. Membrane contactor pilot plant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane contactor pilot plant was located at a black coal fi red power station in New South Wales, Australia, and designed to undertake post-combustion CO2 capture through solvent absorption with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA). The pilot plant was designed by the University of Melbourne, constructed by Furnace Engineering (Australia), installed by the Commonwealth Scienti fi c and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO), commissioned and operated by University of Melbourne researchers. The plant is designed to process up to 20 kg/hr of fl ue gas (composition in Table 1), utilizing 30 wt%
texts.text: The membrane contactor pilot plant was located at a black coal fi red power station in New South Wales, Australia, and designed to undertake post-combustion CO2 capture through solvent absorption with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA). The pilot plant was designed by the University of Melbourne, constructed by Furnace Engineering (Australia), installed by the Commonwealth Scienti fi c and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO), commissioned and operated by University of Melbourne researchers. The plant is designed to process up to 20 kg/hr of fl ue gas (composition in Table 1), utilizing 30 wt%

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MEA circulating with a fl owrate of 1 -2 L/min through both membrane contactors. To provide fl exibility in the process, the lean and rich solvents had storage between the unit operations (Fig. 1). Trace heating was applied to both the fl ue gas and lean solvent entry for the absorption contactor, while an electrical solvent heater was present for the rich solvent before the regenerator contactor.
texts.text: MEA circulating with a fl owrate of 1 -2 L/min through both membrane contactors. To provide fl exibility in the process, the lean and rich solvents had storage between the unit operations (Fig. 1). Trace heating was applied to both the fl ue gas and lean solvent entry for the absorption contactor, while an electrical solvent heater was present for the rich solvent before the regenerator contactor.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The fl ue gas was pretreated with a contact cooler to remove ash and lower the temperature to ambient. Solvent regeneration was achieved through a steam sweep, that both heated the solvent to high temperature and reduced the partial pressure of CO2 on the permeate side of the membrane. Photos of the commissioned pilot plant are provided in Fig. 2.
texts.text: The fl ue gas was pretreated with a contact cooler to remove ash and lower the temperature to ambient. Solvent regeneration was achieved through a steam sweep, that both heated the solvent to high temperature and reduced the partial pressure of CO2 on the permeate side of the membrane. Photos of the commissioned pilot plant are provided in Fig. 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solvent, 30 wt% MEA, was chosen as this solvent has been well characterized and is the industry standard for CO2 solvent absorption. Hence, the performance of the membrane contactor pilot plant could be directly correlated with conventional solvent absorption processes. The steam was generated onsite by a designated boiler, providing saturated steam at 172 °C, which was subsequently reduced to < 105 °C for the membrane module. The operating temperature range of the lean solvent exiting the module at steady-state was between 90 and 102 °C. This enabled solvent regeneration to occur without a bulk solvent phase change while also minimising potential heat damage to the membrane module. The entire process was controlled and monitored with a designated PLC (Factory Talk View), and CO2 concentration in fl ue gas and steam sweep analysed by an inline gas analyser, while lean and rich solvent CO2 loadings, as well as MEA concentration, were determined through FTIR spectrometry.
texts.text: The solvent, 30 wt% MEA, was chosen as this solvent has been well characterized and is the industry standard for CO2 solvent absorption. Hence, the performance of the membrane contactor pilot plant could be directly correlated with conventional solvent absorption processes. The steam was generated onsite by a designated boiler, providing saturated steam at 172 °C, which was subsequently reduced to < 105 °C for the membrane module. The operating temperature range of the lean solvent exiting the module at steady-state was between 90 and 102 °C. This enabled solvent regeneration to occur without a bulk solvent phase change while also minimising potential heat damage to the membrane module. The entire process was controlled and monitored with a designated PLC (Factory Talk View), and CO2 concentration in fl ue gas and steam sweep analysed by an inline gas analyser, while lean and rich solvent CO2 loadings, as well as MEA concentration, were determined through FTIR spectrometry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The contactor module undertaking absorption had an area that is a fi fth of the module undertaking solvent regeneration. This di ff erence in area meant that the solvent volume processed by the absorber module is less than the solvent volume that was processed by the regenerator module. This discrepancy in solvent volume was handled throughout the trials by operating the absorption process continually for a 5-day period, while the regenerator module was operated for only the fi nal 2 days of that 5-day period, with the rich solvent accumulating in a designated tank for the fi rst 3 days. After the 5 day cycle the process began again. This approach was necessary to enable the absorber module to generate enough CO2 rich solvent to ensure the regenerator module could operate at the required fl owrate for the 2 days of operation.
texts.text: The contactor module undertaking absorption had an area that is a fi fth of the module undertaking solvent regeneration. This di ff erence in area meant that the solvent volume processed by the absorber module is less than the solvent volume that was processed by the regenerator module. This discrepancy in solvent volume was handled throughout the trials by operating the absorption process continually for a 5-day period, while the regenerator module was operated for only the fi nal 2 days of that 5-day period, with the rich solvent accumulating in a designated tank for the fi rst 3 days. After the 5 day cycle the process began again. This approach was necessary to enable the absorber module to generate enough CO2 rich solvent to ensure the regenerator module could operate at the required fl owrate for the 2 days of operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The measured CO2 absorption fl ux from the fl ue gas into the 30 wt% MEA solvent across the membrane contactor is provided in Fig. 4. For the 29 days of operation the CO2 fl ux was essentially constant at 2.3 × 10 -3 mol/m 2 s. This steady state performance ensured the CO2 recovery from the fl ue gas was above 90%; achieved through control of the solvent fl owrate and fl ue gas pressure regulation. This fl ux is almost double that observed in the porous PEEK membrane contactor pilot plant trial reported in the literature [17]. This di ff erence is due to the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient for absorption calculated from this data over the 29 days is provided in Fig. 5. The initial overall mass transfer coe ffi cient was ~0.09 ± 0.005 cm/s, but decreased over the operating period to ~0.08 cm/s. This decreased was attributed to dilution of the MEA solvent concentration which fell from 5.04 to 3.23 ± 0.05 mol/L over the operating period. This solvent dilution was due to the regeneration process and is discussed further in Section 4.2. The dilution of the solvent reduced the enhancement factor for the CO2 -MEA reaction (Eq. (3)) and hence decreased the observed solvent boundary layer mass transfer coe ffi cient, which reduced the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient, given it is a rate limiting stage for the absorption process [12]. The overall volumetric mass transfer coe ffi cient for the absorption process, based on surface area per volume, is 5.4 s -1 ; which is considerably greater than that reported for the porous PEEK membrane with MDEA solvent [17]. This substantial di ff erence is due to the denser packing of the PDMS module trialled in this study (60 cm -1 versus 26 cm -1 ) as well as the greater solvent velocity and enhanced reaction kinetics for MEA solvent.
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient for absorption calculated from this data over the 29 days is provided in Fig. 5. This solvent dilution was due to the regeneration process and is discussed further in Section 4.2. The dilution of the solvent reduced the enhancement factor for the CO2 -MEA reaction (Eq. (3)) and hence decreased the observed solvent boundary layer mass transfer coe ffi cient, which reduced the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient, given it is a rate limiting stage for the absorption process [12]. The overall volumetric mass transfer coe ffi cient for the absorption process, based on surface area per volume, is 5.4 s -1 ; which is considerably greater than that reported for the porous PEEK membrane with MDEA solvent [17]. This substantial di ff erence is due to the denser packing of the PDMS module trialled in this study (60 cm -1 versus 26 cm -1 ) as well as the greater solvent velocity and enhanced reaction kinetics for MEA solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: had a loading of ~0.4 mol CO2/mol MEA, which is comparable to a traditional solvent absorption column. As the campaign progressed, the solvent loading decreased to 0.3 mol CO2/mol MEA. This decrease was a direct result of an increase in the solvent fl owrate through the module, in response to solvent dilution, which reduced its residence time and thus capacity to load CO2. The increased in solvent Reynolds Number had no observable impact on the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient (Fig. 5).
texts.text: had a loading of ~0.4 mol CO2/mol MEA, which is comparable to a traditional solvent absorption column. As the campaign progressed, the solvent loading decreased to 0.3 mol CO2/mol MEA. This decrease was a direct result of an increase in the solvent fl owrate through the module, in response to solvent dilution, which reduced its residence time and thus capacity to load CO2. texts.label: text
texts.orig: No degradation of the membrane was observed for the absorption process and no solvent was observed entrained in the fl ue gas, with the solvent mass balance indicated no loss of solvent to the gas phase. This highlights the bene fi ts of a non-porous membrane, as this pilot plant was able to avoid pore wetting and entrainment issues observed in early pilot plants operated by the authors [15,16]. Similarly, no ash was observed within the lumen entry. Subsequently, the pressure drop experienced by the fl ue gas through the module did not change over the operating period. This implied that there was no fouling of the membrane on the gas side, which was attributed to the pretreatment contact cooler cleaning of the fl ue gas.
texts.text: No degradation of the membrane was observed for the absorption process and no solvent was observed entrained in the fl ue gas, with the solvent mass balance indicated no loss of solvent to the gas phase. This highlights the bene fi ts of a non-porous membrane, as this pilot plant was able to avoid pore wetting and entrainment issues observed in early pilot plants operated by the authors [15,16]. Similarly, no ash was observed within the lumen entry. Subsequently, the pressure drop experienced by the fl ue gas through the module did not change over the operating period. This implied that there was no fouling of the membrane on the gas side, which was attributed to the pretreatment contact cooler cleaning of the fl ue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient within the pilot plant contactor module is compared to results previously reported in the literature for CO2 absorption in Fig. 7 as a function of solvent Reynolds Number. The pilot plant performance is equivalent to laboratory literature results, implying operational conditions on the pilot plant were optimal. This was achieved based on learnings about the pilot plant operations during commissioning and prior pilot plant experience with membrane contactor technology. In addition, the non-porous nature of the membrane eliminated pore wetting and solvent entrainment, a major cause for performance loss, while the larger module ensured no end e ff ects infl uenced boundary layer formation and provided laminar fl ow conditions throughout the module.
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient within the pilot plant contactor module is compared to results previously reported in the literature for CO2 absorption in Fig. 7 as a function of solvent Reynolds Number. texts.orig: absorption stage, indicative that both unit operations in the process are initially operating in a complementary manner. However, there is a clear decrease in CO2 fl ux being produced by the module over time. The calculated overall mass transfer coe ffi cient for solvent regeneration is provided in Fig. 9. The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient is signi fi cantly higher than that observed for absorption (Fig. 5), because of the higher temperature of operation and therefore faster di ff usivity of carbamate, CO2 and MEA species through the respective mass transfer stages. The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient remains relatively constant over the trial period at 0.4 cm/s (a volumetric coe ffi cient of 2.9 s -1 ) implying that there is no change to the transfer mechanisms through the membrane and porous support, hence the PDMS based membrane withstood the conditions of solvent regeneration. Therefore, the decrease in CO2 fl ux observed is due to the dilution of MEA in the solvent (Fig. 10). This reduced the total CO2 concentration in the solvent and consequently the CO2 fl ux decreased. The MEA concentration falls by almost 2 mol/L

texts.label: text
texts.orig: over the operating period. This signi fi cant dilution is attributed two factors, water from the steam sweep crossing the membrane due to the di ff erence in water vapour pressure between the hot steam (105 °C) and cooler solvent (90 -102 °C) at the entry of the module; and MEA evaporating from the solvent into the steam sweep. The condensed steam sweep leaving the module had a small amount of MEA present. To minimise solvent dilution, it was necessary to control the sweep fl owrate and pressure, balancing the requirement to retain an adequate temperature pro fi le within the module and steam sweep fl owrate to remove CO2 from the solvent while minimising MEA loss.
texts.text: over the operating period. This signi fi cant dilution is attributed two factors, water from the steam sweep crossing the membrane due to the di ff erence in water vapour pressure between the hot steam (105 °C) and cooler solvent (90 -102 °C) at the entry of the module; and MEA evaporating from the solvent into the steam sweep. The condensed steam sweep leaving the module had a small amount of MEA present. To minimise solvent dilution, it was necessary to control the sweep fl owrate and pressure, balancing the requirement to retain an adequate temperature pro fi le within the module and steam sweep fl owrate to remove CO2 from the solvent while minimising MEA loss.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ideal steam sweep fl owrate to ensure solvent regeneration while minimizing solvent dilution was found to corresponding to a steam sweep of 5 -6 kg/hr within the lumen (Reynolds Number range 4.8 -5.7). However, there was signi fi cant di ffi culty in controlling the membrane module at this steam fl owrate, which in fl uenced the temperature pro fi le within the module and hence impacted the amount of CO2 being liberated from the solvent. This was due to thermal losses from the inadequately insulated module. The solvent concentration only became critically low after 9 days (5 weeks) of operation.
texts.text: The ideal steam sweep fl owrate to ensure solvent regeneration while minimizing solvent dilution was found to corresponding to a steam sweep of 5 -6 kg/hr within the lumen (Reynolds Number range 4.8 -5.7). However, there was signi fi cant di ffi culty in controlling the membrane module at this steam fl owrate, which in fl uenced the temperature pro fi le within the module and hence impacted the amount of CO2 being liberated from the solvent. This was due to thermal losses from the inadequately insulated module. The solvent concentration only became critically low after 9 days (5 weeks) of operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient performance of the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent regeneration as a function of solvent Reynolds Number is provided in Fig. 11. Also included is the laboratory performance of the PDMS membrane undertaking the same solvent regeneration under controlled temperature conditions, with a N2 sweep gas. Given the temperatures in the pilot plant stripper (<105 °C) there is clear evidence of better performance than in the laboratory work. This is attributed to increased fi ber packing density and stable fl ow regimes (both solvent and sweep gas) within the longer and wider module.
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient performance of the membrane contactor module undertaking solvent regeneration as a function of solvent Reynolds Number is provided in Fig. 11. Also included is the laboratory performance of the PDMS membrane undertaking the same solvent regeneration under controlled temperature conditions, with a N2 sweep gas. Given the temperatures in the pilot plant stripper (<105 °C) there is clear evidence of better performance than in the laboratory work. This is attributed to increased fi ber packing density and stable fl ow regimes (both solvent and sweep gas) within the longer and wider module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post-campaign inspection of the membrane contactor module consisted of dismantling of the module and visual inspection of the hollow fi ber bundle, adhesive seal as well as module o-rings and module interior. This inspection indicated that there was no degradation of the membrane or module, with all losses in performance attributed to solvent dilution. Critically, the membrane hollow fi bers retained their o ff white colour, indicating that no MEA induced chemical degradation had occurred over the trial period. Fig. 12 shows the lumen steam sweep entry of the module, where the module, adhesive and o-ring appear undamaged. However, it should be made clear that the steam -vacuum conditions were designed to ensure the operating temperature did not go above the manufacturer ' s speci fi cations for extended periods.

This inspection indicated that there was no degradation of the membrane or module, with all losses in performance attributed to solvent dilution. Critically, the membrane hollow fi bers retained their o ff white colour, indicating that no MEA induced chemical degradation had occurred over the trial period. Fig. 12 shows the lumen steam sweep entry of the module, where the module, adhesive and o-ring appear undamaged. However, it should be made clear that the steam -vacuum conditions were designed to ensure the operating temperature did not go above the manufacturer ' s speci fi cations for extended periods.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Energy duty
texts.text: 4.3. Energy duty

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane contactor pilot plant is not a fully integrated process and as such the total energy duty of pilot plant is high at 9.4 ( ± 1.2) MJ/kg of CO2 capture (electrical and thermal), compared to conventional approaches (less than 3.7 MJ/kg of CO2) [1]. This energy duty is primarily associated with the sensible heat required to raise the stored solvent (lean and rich) from ambient temperature up to absorption and regeneration temperatures, as well as thermal losses through the inadequately insulated regenerator membrane module. The steam generation is considered in the energy duty, but in a large-scale membrane contactor process low value steam from the power station boiler would be used, which would be considered energy neutral for the contactor process.
texts.text: The membrane contactor pilot plant is not a fully integrated process and as such the total energy duty of pilot plant is high at 9.4 ( ± 1.2) MJ/kg of CO2 capture (electrical and thermal), compared to conventional approaches (less than 3.7 MJ/kg of CO2) [1]. This energy duty is primarily associated with the sensible heat required to raise the stored solvent (lean and rich) from ambient temperature up to absorption and regeneration temperatures, as well as thermal losses through the inadequately insulated regenerator membrane module. The steam generation is considered in the energy duty, but in a large-scale membrane contactor process low value steam from the power station boiler would be used, which would be considered energy neutral for the contactor process.

texts.orig: transfer and heat of reaction from the heater and steam to the solvent during regeneration was 3.6 MJ/kg of CO2 (thermal). This represents an energy duty of ~4.2 MJ/kg of CO2 (electrical and thermal) for the membrane contactor process, Energy integration, including the removal of solvent storage and the addition of a cross heat exchanger (lean and rich solvent) would reduce this loss, as would the use of better insulated modules and pipework against thermal losses.

This represents an energy duty of ~4.2 MJ/kg of CO2 (electrical and thermal) for the membrane contactor process, Energy integration, including the removal of solvent storage and the addition of a cross heat exchanger (lean and rich solvent) would reduce this loss, as would the use of better insulated modules and pipework against thermal losses.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pilot plant trials of commercially available non-porous membrane gas-solvent contactors for post-combustion CO2 capture were successfully undertaken. The process utilised membrane contactors for both the absorption and solvent regeneration stages, with a steam sweep used during regeneration. The membranes were based on a poly dimethylsiloxane active layer on a porous polysulfone, coupled with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent. The pilot plant process achieved steady-state CO2 absorption at laboratory comparable overall mass transfer coe ffi cients for the 29 days of operation, with some loss in performance attributed to the dilution of the solvent over time. Importantly, there was no evidence of solvent entrainment in the exiting fl ue gas as well as no evidence of membrane degradation for both unit operations. The overall energy duty of the pilot plant process was high compared to conventional solvent absorption, which was attributed to the process not being energy integrated and su ff ering signi fi cant thermal losses, especially through the membrane modules.
texts.text: Pilot plant trials of commercially available non-porous membrane gas-solvent contactors for post-combustion CO2 capture were successfully undertaken. The process utilised membrane contactors for both the absorption and solvent regeneration stages, with a steam sweep used during regeneration. The membranes were based on a poly dimethylsiloxane active layer on a porous polysulfone, coupled with 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA) solvent. The pilot plant process achieved steady-state CO2 absorption at laboratory comparable overall mass transfer coe ffi cients for the 29 days of operation, with some loss in performance attributed to the dilution of the solvent over time. Importantly, there was no evidence of solvent entrainment in the exiting fl ue gas as well as no evidence of membrane degradation for both unit operations. The overall energy duty of the pilot plant process was high compared to conventional solvent absorption, which was attributed to the process not being energy integrated and su ff ering signi fi cant thermal losses, especially through the membrane modules.
